name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
لِّكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنَا مَنسَكًا هُمْ نَاسِكُوهُ ۖ فَلَا يُنَـٰزِعُنَّكَ فِى ٱلْأَمْرِ ۚ وَٱدْعُ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ ۖ إِنَّكَ لَعَلَىٰ هُدًى مُّسْتَقِيمٍ (٦٧)
```

```english
For every nation We have appointed rites [of worship] which they observe; so let them not dispute with you concerning your religion, and invite to your Lord. Indeed, you are on a straight guidance. (67)
```

```arabic
وَإِن جَـٰدَلُوكَ فَقُلِ ٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (٦٨)
```

```english
But if they dispute with you, say, ‘Allah knows best what you are doing. (68)
```

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَكُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ (٦٩)
```

```english
Allah will judge between you on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which you used to differ. (69)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ فِى كِتَـٰبٍ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ (٧٠)
```

```english
Do you not know that Allah knows whatever there is in the heaven and the earth? All that is in a Book. That is indeed easy for Allah.’ (70)
```

```arabic
وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِۦ سُلْطَـٰنًا وَمَا لَيْسَ لَهُم بِهِۦ عِلْمٌ ۗ وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِن نَّصِيرٍ (٧١)
```

```english
They worship besides Allah that for which He has not sent down any authority, and of which they have no knowledge. The wrongdoers will have no helper. (71)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتُنَا بَيِّنَـٰتٍ تَعْرِفُ فِى وُجُوهِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ٱلْمُنكَرَ ۖ يَكَادُونَ يَسْطُونَ بِٱلَّذِينَ يَتْلُونَ عَلَيْهِمْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا ۗ قُلْ أَفَأُنَبِّئُكُم بِشَرٍّ مِّن ذَٰلِكُمُ ۗ ٱلنَّارُ وَعَدَهَا ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۖ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (٧٢)
```

```english
When Our manifest signs are recited to them, you perceive denial on the faces of the faithless: they would almost pounce upon those who recite Our signs to them. Say, ‘Shall I inform you about something worse than that? The Fire which Allah has promised the faithless, and it is an evil destination.’ (72)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلنَّاسُ ضُرِبَ مَثَلٌ فَٱسْتَمِعُوا۟ لَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ لَن يَخْلُقُوا۟ ذُبَابًا وَلَوِ ٱجْتَمَعُوا۟ لَهُۥ ۖ وَإِن يَسْلُبْهُمُ ٱلذُّبَابُ شَيْـًٔا لَّا يَسْتَنقِذُوهُ مِنْهُ ۚ ضَعُفَ ٱلطَّالِبُ وَٱلْمَطْلُوبُ (٧٣)
```

```english
O people! Listen to a parable that is being drawn: Indeed those whom you invoke besides Allah will never create [even] a fly even if they all rallied to do so! And if a fly should take away something from them, they can not recover that from it. Feeble is the seeker and the sought! (73)
```

```arabic
مَا قَدَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِۦٓ ۗ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَقَوِىٌّ عَزِيزٌ (٧٤)
```

```english
They do not regard Allah with the regard due to Him. Indeed Allah is all-strong, all-mighty. (74)
```

```arabic
ٱللَّهُ يَصْطَفِى مِنَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ رُسُلًا وَمِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (٧٥)
```

```english
Allah chooses messengers from angels and from mankind. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-seeing. (75)
```

```arabic
يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۗ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ ٱلْأُمُورُ (٧٦)
```

```english
He knows that which is before them and that which is behind them, and to Allah all matters are returned. (76)
```

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱرْكَعُوا۟ وَٱسْجُدُوا۟ وَٱعْبُدُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ وَٱفْعَلُوا۟ ٱلْخَيْرَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ ۩ (٧٧)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Bow down and prostrate yourselves, and worship your Lord, and do good, so that you may be felicitous. (77)
```

```arabic
وَجَـٰهِدُوا۟ فِى ٱللَّهِ حَقَّ جِهَادِهِۦ ۚ هُوَ ٱجْتَبَىٰكُمْ وَمَا جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِى ٱلدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ ۚ مِّلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ۚ هُوَ سَمَّىٰكُمُ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ مِن قَبْلُ وَفِى هَـٰذَا لِيَكُونَ ٱلرَّسُولُ شَهِيدًا عَلَيْكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا۟ شُهَدَآءَ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ ۚ فَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ وَءَاتُوا۟ ٱلزَّكَوٰةَ وَٱعْتَصِمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ هُوَ مَوْلَىٰكُمْ ۖ فَنِعْمَ ٱلْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ ٱلنَّصِيرُ (٧٨)
```

```english
And wage jihad for the sake of Allah, a jihad which is worthy of Him. He has chosen you and has not placed for you any obstacle in the religion, the faith of your father, Abraham. He named you ‘Muslims’ before, and in this, so that the Apostle may be a witness to you, and that you may be witnesses to mankind. So maintain the prayer, give the zakat, and hold fast to Allah. He is your master—an excellent master and an excellent helper. (78)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses command him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to call people to Allah and clarify matters regarding the truths of the mission (الدعوة) and the falsehoods of polytheism (الشرك). Then, they command the believers with the sum of the Divine Law (الشريعة), which is the worship of Allah and the performance of good, and conclude with the command regarding the true struggle (الجهاد) for the sake of Allah. With this, the Surah concludes.

**His saying, the Exalted:** لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنَا مَنْسَكًا هُمْ نَاسِكُوهُ فَلَا يُنَازِعُنَّكَ فِي الْأَمْرِ **For every nation We have appointed a rite (منسكا) which they observe (ناسكوه). So do not let them dispute with you concerning the matter.** (22:67) until the end of the verse. The term "rite" (المنسك) is a mim-infinitive meaning "devotion" (النسك), which is worship (العبادة). This is supported by His saying: هُمْ نَاسِكُوهُ — meaning they perform that worship. It is not a noun of place (اسم مكان) as some have supposed.

What is meant by "every nation" (كل أمة) is one nation after another from the past nations until it ends with this nation, rather than the various nations existing during his time (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), such as the Arabs, Persians, and Romans, due to the unity of the Divine Law and the universality of the Prophethood.

His saying: فَلَا يُنَازِعُنَّكَ فِي الْأَمْرِ **So do not let them dispute with you concerning the matter** is a prohibition directed at those who disbelieve in the call of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), forbidding them from disputing with him regarding the rites he brought. Even though they do not believe in his call and do not see any weight in the commands and prohibitions he brought that would necessitate their submission—and there is no effect in prohibiting one who does not acknowledge the authority or lordship of the prohibitor—this prohibition, since it is based on a proof (الحجة), does not become a futile act without effect; that proof is the beginning of the verse.

It is as if the disbelievers among the People of the Book or the polytheists, when they saw Islamic acts of worship for which they had no precedent in previous laws—such as the law of the Jews, for example—disputed with him, asking: "From where did you bring this, when there is no precedent for it in previous laws? If it were from the laws of Prophethood, the believers among the nations of past prophets would have known it." Thus, Allah, the Glorified, answered their dispute with what is in the verse.

The meaning is that each of the nations had a rite (منسك) they observed and a worship they performed, which did not extend to others. This is because Allah, the Glorified, replaced the rite of the predecessors with what was better for the successors, due to their advancement in intellectual maturity (الرقي الفكري) and their readiness in the later stage for what is more complete and superior to the former. Thus, the previous rites are abrogated (منسوخة) for the successors. Therefore, there is no sense in disputing with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding the rite he brought, which differs from the rites of past nations.

Since the prohibition of their disputing with him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) carries the meaning of commanding him to be at peace regarding their dispute and forbidding him from paying it any mind, He followed it with His saying: وَادْعُ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ **and summon to your Lord.** (22:67). It is as if it were said: "Be at peace, do not care for their dispute, and occupy yourself with what you have been commanded, which is the call to your Lord."

He justified this by saying: إِنَّكَ لَعَلَىٰ هُدًى مُسْتَقِيمٍ **Indeed you are on a straight guidance.** (22:67). Describing "guidance" (الهدى) as "straight" (مستقيم)—which is actually an attribute of the "path" (الصراط) toward which one is guided—is a form of intellectual metaphor (المجاز العقلي).

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَإِنْ جَادَلُوكَ فَقُلِ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ **If they dispute with you, say, ‘Allah knows best what you do.’** (22:68). The context of the previous verse supports that what is meant by this "dispute" (الجدال) is arguing and wrangling over the difference between his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) rite and previous laws, after the argument regarding the abrogation of those laws has been established. He (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commanded to refer them back to the judgment of Allah without occupying himself with disputing with them in the same manner they dispute.

It has been said that His saying: إِنْ جَادَلُوكَ **If they dispute with you** refers to any dispute regarding religion in general. It has also been said it refers to the dispute regarding slaughtered animals, but the preceding context does not support this.

His saying: فَقُلِ اللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ **say, ‘Allah knows best what you do’** is a preparation and preamble for referring them to the judgment of Allah. That is: Allah knows best your actions and He will judge with the judgment of one who knows the reality of the situation. He will only judge between you on the Day of Resurrection concerning that which you used to differ in and oppose the truth and its people—"differing" (الاختلاف) and "opposing" (التخالف) carry the same meaning here, like "competing" (الاستباق) and "racing" (التسابق).

**His saying, the Exalted:** أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا فِي السَّمَاءِ وَالْأَرْضِ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ فِي كِتَابٍ ۚ إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ **Do you not know that Allah knows whatever is in the heaven and the earth? That is indeed in a Book. That is indeed easy for Allah.** (22:70). This is a justification for His knowledge of what they do. That is, what they do is part of what is in the heaven and the earth, and He knows all that is in them; therefore, He knows their actions.

His saying: إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ فِي كِتَابٍ **That is indeed in a Book** is an emphasis of what preceded it. That is, whatever He knows is recorded in a Book, so it does not vanish, nor is it forgotten, nor is there any oversight. It is preserved exactly as it is for when He judges between them. His saying: إِنَّ ذَٰلِكَ عَلَى اللَّهِ يَسِيرٌ **That is indeed easy for Allah** means that recording what He knows in a preserved Book is effortless for Him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَيَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ سُلْطَانًا وَمَا لَيْسَ لَهُمْ بِهِ عِلْمٌ **They worship besides Allah that for which He has not sent down any authority (سلطانا), and of which they have no knowledge.** (22:71). The preposition "with" (بـ) in "with it" (به) means "along with." "Authority" (السلطان) means proof (البرهان) and evidence (الحجة). The meaning is: the polytheists worship besides Allah something—which they have taken as a partner to Him—for which Allah has sent down no proof that they might adopt and argue with, nor do they have any knowledge of it.

It is said that He added His saying: وَمَا لَيْسَ لَهُمْ بِهِ عِلْمٌ **and of which they have no knowledge** to His saying: مَا لَمْ يُنَزِّلْ بِهِ سُلْطَانًا **that for which He has not sent down any authority** because a human might know things without a proof or evidence, such as self-evident truths (الضروريات).

The "sending down of authority" has sometimes been interpreted as traditional evidence (الدليل السمعي) and the "existence of knowledge" as rational evidence (الدليل العقلي). That is, they worship besides Allah that for which no evidence stands from the perspective of either revelation or reason. However, there is no evidence for this specific restriction, as the "sending down of authority" applies to the sending of revelation to a prophet just as it applies to the sending of proof into the hearts.

His saying: وَمَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ نَصِيرٍ **And the wrongdoers (للظالمين) shall have no helper.** (22:71). It is said this is a threat to the polytheists, meaning they have no helper to assist them and prevent them from the punishment.

What is apparent—based on what the context provides—is that it is in the position of arguing that they have neither proof for their partners nor knowledge. This is because if they had a proof or knowledge, they would have a helper to assist them, for proof is a helper to the one who argues with it, and knowledge is a helper to the scholar. But they are wrongdoers (ظالمون), and the wrongdoers have no helper; thus, they have neither proof nor knowledge. This is among the most subtle of Quranic arguments.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَإِذا تُتْلى عَلَيْهِمْ آياتُنا بَيِّناتٍ تَعْرِفُ فِي وُجُوهِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا الْمُنْكَرَ يَكادُونَ يَسْطُونَ **When Our manifest signs are recited to them, you will mark denial (المنكر) in the faces of the faithless. They almost pounce...** (22:72). "Denial" (المنكر) is a mim-infinitive meaning "rejection" (الإنكار). What is meant by "marking denial in their faces" is recognizing the effect of rejection and loathing. "They pounce" (يسطون) is from "assault" (السطوة), which, according to *Majma' al-Bayan*, is: "the display of a terrifying state to cause fear; it is said 'he assaulted (سطا) him with an assault (سطوة),' and the person is 'assaulted' (مسطو عليه). Assault (السطوة) and seizing (البطشة) carry the same meaning." End quote.

The meaning is: when Our verses are recited to them while they are clear in their indication, you recognize and witness in the faces of those who disbelieve the mark of rejection. They come close to seizing those who recite Our verses to them because of the rage that overcomes them.

His saying: قُلْ أَفَأُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِشَرٍّ مِنْ ذلِكُمُ **Say, ‘Shall I inform you of something worse than that?’** (22:72) is a derivation from their rejection and their wariness of listening to the Quran. That is: Say, shall I inform you of what is worse than this which you consider an evil to be avoided and whose hearing you guard against? Shall I inform you of it so that you may guard against it, if you are indeed those who guard?

His saying: النَّارُ وَعَدَهَا اللَّهُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا ۖ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ **The Fire, which Allah has promised the faithless! And it is an evil destination.** (22:72) is an explanation of that "evil." That is: that which is worse than this is the Fire. His saying: وَعَدَهَا اللَّهُ **which Allah has promised** etc., is an explanation of why it is worse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ ضُرِبَ مَثَلٌ فَاسْتَمِعُوا لَهُ **O mankind! Listen to a parable (مثل) that is being drawn.** (22:73) until the end of the verse. This is an address to all people, with specific concern for the polytheists among them.

His saying: ضُرِبَ مَثَلٌ فَاسْتَمِعُوا لَهُ **Listen to a parable that is being drawn.** A "parable" (المثل) is a description that represents a thing in a certain state, whether it is a realized, factual description or a hypothetical, imagined one—such as parables involving the dialogues and interactions of animals and inanimate objects. "Drawing a parable" (ضرب المثل) is to set it up so that it may be reflected upon, much like "striking a tent" (ضرب الخيمة) so that one may dwell in it.

This parable is His saying: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ لَنْ يَخْلُقُوا ذُباباً وَلَوِ اجْتَمَعُوا لَهُ وَإِنْ يَسْلُبْهُمُ الذُّبابُ شَيْئاً لا يَسْتَنْقِذُوهُ مِنْهُ **Indeed those whom you invoke besides Allah will never create a fly even if they rallied together for it. And if a fly should take away something from them, they cannot take it back from it.** (22:73). The meaning is that if it were supposed that their gods wished to create a fly—which is the weakest of animals in their view—they would never be able to do so. And if the fly were to snatch something from what is upon them, they could not rescue it by taking it back from the fly.

This description represents the state of their gods besides Allah in their power to bring into existence (الإيجاد) and to govern affairs (تدبير الأمر), as they are unable to create a fly or to manage the simplest of matters, which is recovering what a fly took from them and harmed them with. How then can one who is in such a state deserve to be called upon and worshipped?

His saying: ضَعُفَ الطَّالِبُ وَالْمَطْلُوبُ **Feeble are the seeker and the sought!** (22:73). The context requires that the "seeker" (الطالب) refers to the gods, which are the idols being called upon, for the premise is that they seek to create the fly but cannot, and seek to rescue what it snatched from them but cannot. The "sought" (المطلوب) is the fly, as it is sought to be created and sought so that things may be rescued from it.

In this sentence is an explanation of the ultimate degree of their weakness, for they are weaker than the weakest animal that people deem feeble among those that possess some measure of perception and power.

**His saying, the Exalted:** مَا قَدَرُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ ۗ إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَقَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ **They do not regard Allah with the regard (قدر) due to Him. Indeed Allah is All-strong, All-mighty.** (22:74). The "measure" (قدر) of a thing is its proportion and the determination of its quantity. It is used metaphorically for the status of a thing necessitated by its attributes and qualities. It is said: "He regarded the thing with its true regard" (قدر الشيء حق قدره), meaning he placed it in the status it deserves and treated it as is appropriate for it.

Regarding Allah with His true regard (حق القدر) means adhering to what His supreme attributes necessitate and treating Him as He deserves: by taking Him as a Lord with no lord besides Him, and worshipping Him alone with no deity other than Him. However, the polytheists did not regard Him with His true regard, as they did not take Him as their Lord nor did they worship Him; rather, they took idols as lords besides Him and worshipped them instead of Him, while they see that these idols cannot create a fly and can be humiliated by a fly. Thus, they are at the peak of weakness and humiliation. Meanwhile, Allah, the Glorified, is the All-strong (القوي), the All-mighty (العزيز), to Whom all creation and command return, and He is the One Who sustains existence and governance.

So His saying: مَا قَدَرُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ قَدْرِهِ **They do not regard Allah with the regard due to Him** is an allusion to their failure to adhere to His Lordship and their turning away from His worship, and then their taking idols as lords besides Him, worshipping them out of fear and hope instead of Allah.

His saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَقَوِيٌّ عَزِيزٌ **Indeed Allah is All-strong, All-mighty** is a justification for the preceding negation. He has mentioned "strength" (القوة) and "might" (العزة) in absolute terms, indicating that He is strong such that no weakness affects Him, and mighty such that no humiliation touches Him, as He said: أَنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا **that power belongs entirely to Allah** (2:165), and He said: فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا **for all might belongs to Allah** (4:139). These two names were specifically mentioned to contrast with the attributes of their gods in the drawn parable, which are weakness and humiliation. These people belittled the matter of their Lord when they equated Him—the All-strong Who creates what He wills and the All-mighty Whom nothing overcomes nor humiliates—with idols and gods who are too weak to create a fly and are humiliated by a fly. Then they were not satisfied with that until they prioritized them over Him, taking them as lords to be worshipped instead of Him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** اللَّهُ يَصْطَفِي مِنَ الْمَلَائِكَةِ رُسُلًا وَمِنَ النَّاسِ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ **Allah chooses (يصطفي) messengers from the angels and from men. Indeed Allah is All-hearing, All-seeing.** (22:75). "Choosing" (الاصطفاء) is taking the purest (صفوة) and finest part of a thing. Al-Raghib said: "Choosing (الاصطفاء) is taking the pure part (صفو) of a thing, just as 'selecting' (الاختيار) is taking its best (خير), and 'electing' (الاجتباء) is taking its collection (جباية)." End quote.

Thus, Allah’s choosing messengers from the angels and from men is His selection from among them of those who are pure for that purpose and fit for it.

This verse and the one following it clarify the necessity of establishing the messengership, its nature, and the attribute of the messengers, which is infallibility (العصمة). This discourse has some connection to His previous saying: لِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ جَعَلْنَا مَنْسَكًا هُمْ نَاسِكُوهُ **For every nation We have appointed a rite which they observe** because of its indication of messengership.

The verse clarifies, first, that Allah has messengers from the angels and from men; and second, that this messengership is not haphazard or from anyone at random, but rather through choosing (الاصطفاء) and the designation of one who is fit for it.

His saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ **Indeed Allah is All-hearing, All-seeing** is a justification for the principle of sending messengers. For people—meaning the human species—have a primordial need (حاجة فطرية) for Allah, the Glorified, to guide them toward their happiness and the perfection intended by their creation, just like all other species in existence. Thus, the need for guidance (الهداية) is universal. The manifestation of need in them—or if you wish, their manifestation of need from themselves—is a request from them and a petition for that which would remove their need. Allah, the Glorified, is All-hearing and All-seeing; He sees with His sight the primordial need for guidance they are in, and He hears with His hearing their petition for that.

Therefore, the requirement of His hearing and sight is that He sends a messenger to them and guides them through him to their happiness, which they were created to attain and embody. Not all people are fit for connection with the Realm of Sanctity (عالم القدس), for among them are the wicked and the good, the corrupt and the righteous. The messenger is of two types: an angelic messenger who receives the revelation from Him and delivers it to the human messenger, and a human messenger who receives the revelation from the angelic messenger and conveys it to the people. In short, His saying: إِنَّ اللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ **Indeed Allah is All-hearing, All-seeing** contains the proof for the necessity of the principle of sending messengers. As for the meaning of "choosing" (الاصطفاء) and the proof for its necessity, it is what His saying points to: يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ **He knows what is before them and what is behind them.**

**His saying, the Exalted:** يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ ۗ وَإِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ **He knows what is before them and what is behind them, and to Allah all matters are returned.** (22:76). The apparent context is that the plural pronoun in both places refers to the messengers from the angels and men. This is evidenced by the occurrence of this expression regarding them in other places, such as His saying, recounting the words of the angels of revelation: وَمَا نَتَنَزَّلُ إِلَّا بِأَمْرِ رَبِّكَ ۖ لَهُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِينَا وَمَا خَلْفَنَا **We do not descend except by the command of your Lord. To Him belongs whatever is before us and whatever is behind us** (19:64), and His saying: فَلَا يُظْهِرُ عَلَىٰ غَيْبِهِ أَحَدًا إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضَىٰ مِنْ رَسُولٍ فَإِنَّهُ يَسْلُكُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَمِنْ خَلْفِهِ رَصَدًا لِيَعْلَمَ أَنْ قَدْ أَبْلَغُوا رِسَالَاتِ رَبِّهِمْ وَأَحَاطَ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ **He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves of. Then He dispatches a sentinel before him and behind him, that He may know that they have delivered the messages of their Lord, and He encompasses all that is with them** (72:26-28).

The verse—as you see—proclaims that the mention of His knowledge of what is before them and what is behind them is to indicate that He is monitoring the path taken by the revelation between Him and the people, guarding it against any internal defect through forgetfulness or alteration, or any external corruption through the plots and suggestions of the devils. All this is because the carriers of revelation among the messengers are under His very eye and in His presence; He knows what is before them and what is behind them, and He is ever-watchful.

From here it becomes clear that what is meant by "what is before them" (ما بين أيديهم) is what is between them and those to whom they deliver. Thus, what is before the angelic messenger is what is between him and the human messenger, and what is before the human messenger is what is between him and the people. What is meant by "what is behind them" (ما خلفهم) is what is between them and Allah, the Glorified. All are traveling from the side of Allah toward the people.

Thus, the revelation is in a divine sanctuary from the moment it issues from the Presence of Grandeur and Majesty until it reaches the people. The necessary consequence of this is that the messengers are infallible (معصومون) in receiving the revelation, infallible in preserving it, and infallible in conveying it to the people.

His saying: وَإِلَى اللَّهِ تُرْجَعُ الْأُمُورُ **and to Allah all matters are returned** is in the position of justifying His knowledge of what is before them and what is behind them. That is: how could anything of that be hidden from Him when all matters return to Him? Since this return is not a temporal return (رجوعا زمانيا)—which would allow for the state of the thing to be hidden before its return—but rather its very essence is owned by Him, it has no independence from Him and nothing in it is hidden from Him. Understand this.

**His saying, the Exalted:** يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ارْكَعُوا وَاسْجُدُوا وَاعْبُدُوا رَبَّكُمْ وَافْعَلُوا الْخَيْرَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ **O you who have faith! Bow down and prostrate yourselves, and worship your Lord, and do good, so that you may be felicitous.** (22:77). The command to bow and prostrate is a command to perform prayer (الصلاة). The requirement of contrast is that what is meant by His saying: وَاعْبُدُوا رَبَّكُمْ **and worship your Lord** is the command for all other acts of worship legislated in the religion, such as Hajj and fasting. This leaves for His saying: وَافْعَلُوا الْخَيْرَ **and do good** all other legislated rulings and laws, for in their establishment and practice lies the good of society and the happiness and life of individuals, as He said: اسْتَجِيبُوا لِلَّهِ وَلِلرَّسُولِ إِذَا دَعَاكُمْ لِمَا يُحْيِيكُمْ **Answer Allah and the Apostle when he summons you to that which will give you life** (8:24).

In the verse is a command encompassing the sum of Islamic laws, including acts of worship and others.

**His saying, the Exalted:** وَجَاهِدُوا فِي اللَّهِ حَقَّ جِهَادِهِ **And strive (جاهِدوا) in the cause of Allah with a striving (جهاد) that is worthy of Him.** (22:78) until the end of the verse. "Struggle" (الجهاد) is to expend effort and exhaust one's capacity in repelling the enemy. It is most often used for repelling through fighting (القتال), but sometimes the meaning of "enemy" is expanded to include everything from which evil is expected, such as the devil who misleads man, the soul that commands to evil (النفس الأمارة بالسوء), and so on. Thus, the term is applied to opposing the soul in its desires and avoiding obedience to the devil in his whisperings. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) called the opposition of the soul the "greater struggle" (جهادا أكبر).

It appears that what is meant by "struggle" (الجهاد) in the verse is the broader meaning, especially considering its qualification by His saying: فِي اللَّهِ **in Allah**, which refers to everything that returns to Him. This is also supported by His saying: وَالَّذِينَ جَاهَدُوا فِينَا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنَا **As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them to Our ways** (29:69).

Accordingly, the meaning of the struggle being "worthy of Him" (حق جهاده) is that it be purely within the meaning of struggle and sincere for His noble sake, with no one else sharing in it. This is similar to "fearing Allah as He should be feared" (تقوى الله حق تقواه) in His saying: اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ حَقَّ تُقَاتِهِ **be wary of Allah with the wariness due to Him** (3:102).

His saying: هُوَ اجْتَبَاكُمْ وَمَا جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ **He has chosen you (اجتباكم) and has not placed for you any obstacle (حرج) in religion** (22:78) is a favor from Him upon the believers, indicating that they would not have attained the happiness of religion by themselves or through their own power; rather, Allah bestowed His favor upon them by granting them success, choosing them, and gathering them for the religion. He removed every "obstacle" (حرج) in the religion as a favor, whether it be an obstacle in the original ruling or an obstacle that arises incidentally. Thus, it is a simple and tolerant law, the creed of your father Abraham, the upright (الحنيف), who submitted to his Lord.

Abraham was called the father of the Muslims because he (عليه السلام) was the first to submit to Allah, as the Exalted said: إِذْ قَالَ لَهُ رَبُّهُ أَسْلِمْ ۖ قَالَ أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ **When his Lord said to him, ‘Submit,’ he said, ‘I submit to the Lord of all the worlds’** (2:131). He also said, recounting his (عليه السلام) words: فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي **so whoever follows me indeed belongs to me** (14:36), thus attributing his followers to himself. He also said: وَاجْنُبْنِي وَبَنِيَّ أَنْ نَعْبُدَ الْأَصْنامَ **and keep me and my sons away from worshipping idols** (14:35), and his meaning by "his sons" is certainly the Muslims, not the polytheists. He also said: إِنَّ أَوْلَى النَّاسِ بِإِبْرَاهِيمَ لَلَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ وَهَٰذَا النَّبِيُّ وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا **Indeed the nearest of all people to Abraham are those who followed him, and this prophet and those who have faith** (3:68).

His saying: هُوَ سَمَّاكُمُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ مِنْ قَبْلُ وَفِي هَٰذَا **He named you ‘Muslims’ before and in this** (22:78) is a second favor from Him upon the believers after the favor in His saying: هُوَ اجْتَبَاكُمْ **He has chosen you**. The pronoun refers to Allah, the Exalted. His saying: مِنْ قَبْلُ **before** means before the revelation of the Quran, and His saying: وَفِي هَٰذَا **and in this** means in this Book. In His favor upon them by mentioning that He named them Muslims is an indication of His acceptance of their submission (إسلامهم).

His saying: لِيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ شَهِيدًا عَلَيْكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا شُهَدَاءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ **so that the Apostle may be a witness to you, and that you may be witnesses to mankind.** (22:78). What is meant by this is the witnessing of deeds (شهادة الأعمال). The discussion on the meaning of this verse has already preceded in Surah al-Baqarah, verse 143, and elsewhere. In the verse is a justification for what preceded regarding the choosing (الاجتباء), the negation of obstacles (نفي الحرج), and naming them Muslims.

His saying: فَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ وَاعْتَصِمُوا بِاللَّهِ **So maintain the prayer and give the zakat, and hold fast to Allah.** (22:78) is a derivation from all that preceded of the favors He bestowed upon them. That is: based on this, it is obligatory for you to maintain the prayer and give the zakat—which is an allusion to practicing the devotional and financial rulings—and to hold fast to Allah in all circumstances. Thus, you must follow all that He commanded and refrain from all that He forbade, and never disconnect from Him in any state, because He is your Master (مولاكم). It is not for a servant to disconnect from his master in any state, nor for a weak human to disconnect from his helper—based on the two possible meanings of "Master" (المولى).

So His saying: هُوَ مَوْلاكُمْ **He is your Master** is in the position of justifying the preceding ruling. His saying: فَنِعْمَ الْمَوْلَىٰ وَنِعْمَ النَّصِيرُ **What an excellent Master and what an excellent Helper!** (22:78) is a word of praise for Him, the Exalted, and a comforting of the souls of the believers and a strengthening of their hearts, for their Master and Helper is Allah, besides Whom there is no master and no helper.

Know that what we have mentioned regarding the meaning of "choosing" (الاجتباء), as well as "submission" (الإسلام) and others in the verse, is what the majority of commentators have mentioned based on the apparent address "O you who have faith" at the beginning of the discourse and its inclusion of the general body of believers and the entire nation.

However, we have clarified more than once that "choosing" (الاجتباء) in its true sense is synonymous with making the servant "sincere" (مخلصاً)—with a fatha on the lam—exclusively for Allah, such that no one else besides Allah has a share in him. This is an attribute found only in a few numbered individuals of the nation, certainly not in everyone. The same applies to the meaning of "submission" (الإسلام) and "holding fast" (الاعتصام). The meaning in its reality is certainly intended in the discourse.

Accordingly, the attribution of choosing, submission, and witnessing to the entire nation is an expansion (توسع) by virtue of their inclusion of those who are characterized by these attributes in their reality. This is similar to His saying regarding the Children of Israel: وَجَعَلَكُمْ مُلُوكًا **and made you kings** (5:20), and His saying regarding them: وَفَضَّلْنَاهُمْ عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ **and We preferred them over all the nations** (45:16), and many similar examples in the Quran.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Jawami' al-Jami'*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَلَا يُنَازِعُنَّكَ فِي الْأَمْرِ **So do not let them dispute with you concerning the matter** — it is narrated that Budayl ibn Warqa' and others from the disbelievers of Khuza'ah said to the Muslims: "What is the matter with you that you eat what you have killed, but you do not eat what Allah has killed?"—meaning carrion.

**I say:** The context of the verse does not support this.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration to Abd al-Rahman, the seller of mats, from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said:

"The Quraysh used to smear the idols that were around the Kaaba with musk and ambergris. *Yaghuth* was opposite the door, *Ya'uq* was to the right of the Kaaba, and *Nasr* was to its left. When they entered, they would fall down prostrating to *Yaghuth*—and they would not bow—then they would turn around facing *Ya'uq*, then they would turn to the left facing *Nasr*. Then they would recite the *talbiyah*, saying: 'At Your service, O Allah, at Your service; at Your service, You have no partner except a partner who belongs to You; You own him and what he owns.'"

He said: "So Allah sent a green fly with four wings, and it did not leave any of that musk and ambergris without eating it. Then Allah, Mighty and Majestic, revealed: يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ ضُرِبَ مَثَلٌ فَاسْتَمِعُوا لَهُ **O mankind! Listen to a parable that is being drawn...** (22:73)."

In the same source, with its chain of narration to Burayd al-Ijli, who said: I said to Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا ارْكَعُوا وَاسْجُدُوا وَاعْبُدُوا رَبَّكُمْ وَافْعَلُوا الْخَيْرَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُفْلِحُونَ وَجَاهِدُوا فِي اللَّهِ حَقَّ جِهَادِهِ **O you who have faith! Bow down and prostrate yourselves, and worship your Lord, and do good, so that you may be felicitous. And strive in the cause of Allah with a striving that is worthy of Him.** — He said: "He meant us, and we are the chosen ones (المجتبون). Allah, Blessed and Exalted, has not placed for us any obstacle (حرج) in religion—and an obstacle (الحرج) is more severe than a narrowness (الضيق)."

مِلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْرَاهِيمَ **the creed of your father, Abraham** — "He meant us specifically." هُوَ سَمَّاكُمُ الْمُسْلِمِينَ **He named you ‘Muslims’** — "Allah, Mighty and Majestic, named us Muslims." مِنْ قَبْلُ **before** — "in the books that have passed." وَفِي هَٰذَا **and in this** — "the Quran." لِيَكُونَ الرَّسُولُ شَهِيدًا عَلَيْكُمْ وَتَكُونُوا شُهَدَاءَ عَلَى النَّاسِ **so that the Apostle may be a witness to you, and that you may be witnesses to mankind.** — "So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is the witness over us regarding what he delivered to us from Allah, Blessed and Exalted, and we are the witnesses over mankind on the Day of Resurrection. Whomever we testify for on the Day of Resurrection, he is truthful, and whomever we testify against, he is a liar."

**I say:** The narrations from the Shi'a paths from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (peace be upon them) regarding this meaning are numerous. What was mentioned under the verse clarifies the meaning of these narrations.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Hakim—who authenticated it—narrated from Aisha: that she asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) about this verse: وَمَا جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ **and has not placed for you any obstacle in religion** — He said: "Narrowness (الضيق)."

In *al-Tahdhib*, with its chain of narration to Abd al-A'la, the client of the family of Sam, who said: I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): "I tripped and my fingernail was torn off, so I placed a bandage over my finger. How should I perform ablution?" He said: "This and its like are known from the Book of Allah, Mighty and Majestic. Allah said: مَا جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ **He has not placed for you any obstacle in religion** — wipe over it."

**I say:** There are other narrations with this meaning that cite the verse as evidence for the removal of burdensome rulings. The reliance on the verse for the ruling indicates the correctness of what we presented regarding the meaning of the verse.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Shaybah in *al-Musannaf* and Ishaq ibn Rahwayh in his *Musnad* narrated from Makhul that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Allah named me with two names by which He named my nation: He is the Peace (السلام) and He named my nation the Muslims (المسلمين); and He is the Believer (المؤمن) and He named my nation the believers (المؤمنين)."

— Completed, and praise be to Allah —
